name: Prajna (Wisdom)
shortDescription: Prajna is the Buddhist concept of wisdom, insight, and understanding. It is a key component of the path to enlightenment, involving the direct realization of the true nature of reality.
description: Prajna, often translated as wisdom, is a central concept in Buddhism. It represents a profound understanding of reality that goes beyond mere intellectual knowledge. It is not simply knowing facts or theories, but rather a direct, intuitive realization of the true nature of existence, including impermanence (anicca), suffering (dukkha), and non-self (anatta). Prajna is often described as the 'wisdom that sees through' the illusions and delusions that bind beings to the cycle of rebirth (samsara). It is the wisdom that understands emptiness (sunyata), the absence of inherent existence in all phenomena. This understanding leads to the cessation of suffering and the attainment of Nirvana. Prajna is cultivated through meditation, study, and ethical conduct. The Perfection of Wisdom (Prajnaparamita) literature, a central body of Mahayana Buddhist texts, elaborates on the nature of prajna and its importance in the path to enlightenment. It emphasizes the emptiness of all dharmas (phenomena) and the importance of non-attachment. The realization of prajna is considered essential for liberation from suffering and the attainment of Buddhahood. The cultivation of prajna also involves developing skillful means (upaya), the ability to apply wisdom and compassion appropriately in different situations. It is often associated with the bodhisattva Manjushri, who embodies wisdom and is often depicted holding a sword that cuts through ignorance. Prajna works in conjunction with compassion (karuna), forming the two wings that allow a practitioner to fly to enlightenment. Without wisdom, compassion can be misguided; without compassion, wisdom can be cold and detached.

sources.title: Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines
sources.author: Edward Conze (Translator)
